claim: Hand B exhibits the same two-paradigm morphology as Hand A: Layer-1 VOWEL-class inners {i, e, o, a} preferentially take Layer-2 outers {n, r, l}, and CONSONANT-class inners {ch, d, k, t, sh, l} preferentially take Layer-2 outers {y, ol}, with at least one categorical (100% sub-class-exclusive) gap mirroring Hand A's n-exclusivity (0/2240) pattern. Confirmation would indicate Hand A and Hand B share a grammatical substrate despite zero stem overlap — i.e., one language written by two scribes, not two distinct languages.
evidence_for: H-BV-SUFFIX-SUBCLASS-01 (2026-04-16) established a striking complementary pattern in Hand A: VOWEL-inner → {n,r,l} and CONSONANT-inner → {y,ol} with every top-5 outer showing ≥3× differential and 'n' being 0/2240 vowel-exclusive. If Hand A and Hand B share grammar, the same pattern should replicate.

Currier's A/B distinction is conventionally framed as a dialectal / scribal split within a common system — the field's default assumption is that both hands encode the same underlying language. A replication of the SUBCLASS-01 pattern would be a strong quantitative validation of that default.

Hand A and Hand B have near-zero stem overlap at the full-word level (prior Brain-V finding), yet may still share morphological paradigms. Morphological-paradigm sharing without stem sharing is exactly what dialectal variation looks like (e.g., different lexicon, same inflection system).
evidence_against: Currier's original 1976 analysis flagged A and B as having measurably different statistical signatures — if those signatures reflect different grammars, the SUBCLASS-01 pattern may not replicate.

Hand B has a different character-frequency profile from Hand A (higher 'chedy', 'shedy' frequencies, etc.). A morphology bound tightly to character statistics could diverge alongside the character-statistic divergence.

The partition VOWEL {i,e,o,a} / CONSONANT {ch,d,k,t,sh,l} was derived from Hand A's top-10 inner inventory. If Hand B's productive inner inventory differs, the partition is imported from outside Hand B and the test risks measuring fit-of-Hand-A-partition-to-Hand-B rather than Hand B's intrinsic morphology.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE — identical to H-BV-SUFFIX-SUBCLASS-01 but run on Hand B tokens.

  DATA: Hand B tokens (Currier language B folios) under Tokenisation A (MULTI_GLYPHS + 'ol'), N>=3 glyph-units.

  PARTITION (LOCKED, imported from Hand A):
    VOWEL-INNERS    = {i, e, o, a}
    CONSONANT-INNERS = {ch, d, k, t, sh, l}
    TOP-5 OUTERS    = {y, n, r, ol, l}  (Hand A's top-5; the claim specifies this outer set)

  METRICS:

  M1 — ORDERING RATIOS per sub-class S in {VOWEL, CONSONANT}:
    ratio_S = P(outer in TOP-5 | inner in S) / P(outer in TOP-5 | inner NOT in S)
    Comparator 'NOT in S' = all non-S Hand-B inners.

  M2 — 2x5 OUTER PREFERENCE TABLE:
    For each outer o in {y, n, r, ol, l}, compute:
      p_VOWEL(o) = count(inner in VOWEL AND outer = o) / count(inner in VOWEL AND outer in TOP-5 — for direct comparability with Hand A's table use count(inner in VOWEL) as denominator)
      p_CONSONANT(o) analogously
      diff(o) = p_VOWEL(o) / p_CONSONANT(o)
    Flag each outer as:
      '>=3x vowel-preferring' if diff >= 3.0
      '>=3x consonant-preferring' if diff <= 1/3 (and > 0)
      'vowel-exclusive' if p_CONSONANT(o) == 0 AND p_VOWEL(o) > 0
      'consonant-exclusive' if p_VOWEL(o) == 0 AND p_CONSONANT(o) > 0
      'weak (<3x)' otherwise

  M3 — CATEGORICAL-GAP COUNT:
    n_categorical = number of outers in {y,n,r,ol,l} where one sub-class has zero attachments despite the other having >0.

  M4 — DIAGNOSTIC (non-decision-feeding):
    Report Hand B's NATIVE top-10 inner inventory and top-5 outer inventory (without the Hand-A-imported partition), for context. If Hand B's native inventories differ substantially, this diagnoses whether a divergence is about outer selection OR about inventory entirely.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED shared morphology: ALL 5 outers have diff >= 3.0 OR <= 1/3 (every outer is at least 3x sub-class-preferring) AND n_categorical >= 1 (at least one outer fully sub-class-exclusive, mirroring Hand A's n-exclusivity).
    REFUTED Hand-B-differs: ALL 5 outers have diff between 1/2 and 2 (no 3x preferring, no 2x preferring either) AND n_categorical == 0.
    MARGINAL: any intermediate state — e.g., 2-4 outers cross the 3x threshold, or ratios in the 2-3x zone, or partial categorical behaviour on one outer only.
test_metric: per_outer_diff_ratios, n_categorical, plus M1 ordering ratios for reference
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if all 5 outers >=3x differential AND >=1 categorical gap. REFUTED if all 5 outers <2x differential AND 0 categorical gaps. MARGINAL otherwise.
reasoning: H-BV-SUFFIX-SUBCLASS-01 found complementary Layer-1 / Layer-2 selection in Hand A: VOWEL-inner → {n,r,l}, CONSONANT-inner → {y,ol}, with every top-5 outer at >=3x differential and 'n' categorically vowel-exclusive. The natural question is whether this is a Hand-A-specific artifact or a property of the manuscript's grammar. Applying the SAME locked partition and outer set to Hand B tests replication. A positive replication would be strong evidence for shared grammar with dialectal or scribal surface variation — the field's default hypothesis — and would motivate a unified morphological model. A negative replication would split the manuscript into two grammatical systems, strengthening the two-scribe-two-languages interpretation. The categorical-gap criterion ('n' vowel-exclusive) is the sharpest single marker — Hand A's 0/2240 attachment count is not a frequency dip but a categorical ban, and replication of such a ban in Hand B would be particularly hard to explain under a 'two different grammars' model.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed Hand-B replication test, motivated by carbon-dated folio reference set (f8, f26, f47, f68) establishing Hand B's field-anchored provenance
